title: Leucanthemum pectinatum
taxonomy: Leucanthemum pectinatum is a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It belongs to the genus Leucanthemum, which consists of daisy-like plants. The genus Leucanthemum is part of the larger family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. The classification of Leucanthemum pectinatum reflects its botanical relationships and evolutionary history. By grouping it with other daisy-like plants, its classification highlights the shared characteristics and traits of this species.
uses: Leucanthemum pectinatum, also known as the prickly oxeye daisy, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help to soothe skin irritations and reduce swelling. Its leaves and flowers are used in herbal remedies to treat various ailments, including digestive issues and respiratory problems. Leucanthemum pectinatum is also believed to have antimicrobial properties, which can help to prevent the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness of Leucanthemum pectinatum as a medicinal plant.

The plant is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which can help to soothe wounds and reduce inflammation. parts: Leucanthemum pectinatum, also known as the sticky marguerite, is a plant species that has been studied for its potential medicinal properties. intro: Leucanthemum pectinatum, commonly known as the button-leaved daisy, is a perennial herbaceous plant belonging to the Asteraceae family. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, making it useful for treating various conditions such as wounds, digestive issues, and respiratory problems. The bioactive constituents of Leucanthemum pectinatum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpenes, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is often used to make herbal preparations such as infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which are used in traditional medicine to promote healing, reduce inflammation, and support overall well-being.
